framing: Read Hermes' self-evolution as a 5-stage control loop, descriptively (what the code actually implements). README front-loads the biggest claims; the code has a real, elaborate learning apparatus — but the missing gear is exactly the one the hypothesis names: nothing measures whether a self-made change improved outcomes. The loop is open at the credit-assignment stage.

readme_claims_verbatim.verbatim_artifact: The self-improving AI agent built by Nous Research. It's the only agent with a built-in learning loop — it creates skills from experience, improves them during use, nudges itself to persist knowledge, searches its own past conversations, and builds a deepening model of who you are across sessions.

A closed learning loop | Agent-curated memory with periodic nudges. Autonomous skill creation after complex tasks. Skills self-improve during use. FTS5 session search with LLM summarization for cross-session recall.
Research-ready | Batch trajectory generation, trajectory compression for training the next generation of tool-calling models.

fitness.finding: There is no fitness function. In [tools/skill_usage.py, agent/curator.py, agent/background_review.py, batch_runner.py, mini_swe_runner.py] searched, no metric anywhere defines 'good output' at the task level — no reward, pass-rate, correctness score, task-outcome label, or user-rating field. The only quantity that looks like a score, batch_runner's 'success_rate', is a TOOL-CALL success rate (fraction of tool calls that returned without erroring), not a measure of whether the task was solved. So the system optimizes nothing; it accumulates. The one soft objective that exists is embedded in prose: the review prompt tells the model to treat a no-op review as failure ('a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome') — i.e. the implicit objective is WRITE MORE, not GET BETTER.

evidence.claim: The only 'success_rate' is fraction of tool calls that did not error, computed for batch telemetry — not a task-outcome fitness
evidence.verbatim_artifact: stats["success_rate"] = round(stats["success"] / total_calls * 100, 2)

evidence.claim: Skill sidecar stores only activity counters, no outcome/quality field
evidence.verbatim_artifact: "use_count": 0, "view_count": 0, "last_used_at": None, ... "patch_count": 0,

evidence.claim: Implicit objective in the review prompt is volume of writes, not measured improvement
evidence.verbatim_artifact: Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

error_signal.finding: The only 'am I doing badly?' signal is IN-SESSION and TEXTUAL: a background-review fork reads the just-finished transcript and looks for heuristic cues — the user corrected style/tone/format, expressed frustration ('stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'I hate when you Y'), corrected a workflow, or a loaded skill turned out wrong. There is no cross-session or outcome-derived error signal. It is negative-feedback-within-the-turn, detected by an LLM reading its own chat, not a measured error against any target. The nudge that triggers the review is itself interval-based (every N turns / N tool-iterations), not error-triggered — the system doesn't 'notice it's underperforming', it reviews on a clock.
error_signal.status: default-on when memory/skills enabled (shipped config); trigger is interval-based, not error-based

evidence.claim: Error signals are heuristic textual cues the review LLM looks for, not measurements
evidence.verbatim_artifact: User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose' ... are FIRST-CLASS skill signals

evidence.claim: Skill review fires on a tool-iteration interval, not on any error
evidence.verbatim_artifact: if (agent._skill_nudge_interval > 0 and agent._iters_since_skill >= agent._skill_nudge_interval and "skill_manage" in agent.valid_tool_names): _should_review_skills = True

evidence.claim: Memory review fires on a turn-count interval
evidence.verbatim_artifact: if agent._turns_since_memory >= agent._memory_nudge_interval: should_review_memory = True

actuators.name: Skill self-creation / self-patching
actuators.finding: TWO paths, both heuristic-triggered. (1) Foreground: the system prompt nudges the model, after 'a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow', to save/patch a skill. (2) Background: a forked review agent (memory+skill tools only) replays the transcript and writes skills autonomously. The write is real and unattended, but the TRIGGER is 'the task looked complex / a correction happened', never 'this approach measurably raised success'. Strong bias to PATCH EXISTING over create-new: the review prompt's preference order is (1) update a currently-loaded skill, (2) update an existing umbrella, (3) add a support file, (4) create a new umbrella as a last resort — matching the archive note. Create-new is explicitly the fallback.
actuators.status: default-on in shipped config (skill tools in default toolset; nudge interval default 10 in code / 15 in example config); background fork best-effort

evidence.claim: Foreground trigger is a heuristic (complexity/completion), not a measured result
evidence.verbatim_artifact: After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time. When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately ... don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

evidence.claim: Background review fork writes skills/memory autonomously after a turn
evidence.verbatim_artifact: After every turn ... fire off a daemon thread that replays the conversation snapshot in a forked AIAgent and asks itself "should any skill/memory be saved or updated?". Writes go straight to the memory + skill stores.

evidence.claim: Preference order: patch existing first, create new is #4 (fallback)
evidence.verbatim_artifact: 1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL ... 2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA ... 3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE ... 4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class.

actuators.name: Curated memory (MEMORY.md + USER.md)
actuators.finding: Bounded declarative-fact stores injected into the system prompt each session, written by the agent and by the background review. Mechanism detail belongs to Ch05; for this chapter what matters: writes are append-biased. ADD is append-only and explicitly SKIPS the drift guard; the only gate on add is exact-duplicate rejection. Editing/deleting (replace/remove) is what the drift guard protects (#26045). So the asymmetry is baked in: adding is frictionless, removing is guarded — accumulation by construction.
actuators.status: default-off in code (memory_enabled=False), default-on in shipped example config (memory_enabled/user_profile_enabled: true); disabled in batch runs (skip_memory=True)

evidence.claim: add() skips the drift guard; guard only covers replace/remove
evidence.verbatim_artifact: For add (append-only), we skip the drift guard — appending never clobbers existing content ... The drift guard remains active for replace/remove where full-file rewrite would discard un-roundtrippable content (issue #26045).

evidence.claim: Only add-gate is exact-duplicate rejection
evidence.verbatim_artifact:
# Reject exact duplicates
            if content in entries: return self._success_response(target, "Entry already exists (no duplicate added).")

evidence.claim: Code default is off; shipped config turns it on
evidence.verbatim_artifact: agent._memory_enabled = False (code) vs memory_enabled: true / user_profile_enabled: true (example config)

actuators.name: Trajectory dump (save_trajectories)
actuators.finding: An opt-in local dump: appends the finished conversation (ShareGPT format) to trajectory_samples.jsonl (or failed_trajectories.jsonl if not completed) in the working dir. 'completed' means the run finished vs. errored/interrupted — NOT a task-quality label. Default False. Nothing auto-pipes these into the batch/training pipeline.
actuators.status: default-off (opt-in), writes a local file only

evidence.claim: Default False, appends to a local JSONL
evidence.verbatim_artifact: save_trajectories: bool = False ... Save conversation trajectories to JSONL files (appends to trajectory_samples.jsonl). Defaults to False.

evidence.claim: Split into completed vs failed file by run-completion, not quality
evidence.verbatim_artifact: filename = "trajectory_samples.jsonl" if completed else "failed_trajectories.jsonl"

credit_assignment.finding: THE MISSING GEAR — confirmed. After the agent makes a self-modification (writes a memory, creates/patches a skill), NO code measures whether that change made subsequent results better. Three sub-findings prove it: (a) The action summarizer collects 'successful' review actions, where 'successful' means the write tool-call returned OK — it never checks downstream impact. (b) The skill telemetry stores only activity counters (use/view/patch counts + timestamps); there is no success, rating, helpfulness, or outcome field, so a skill that is used constantly but produces bad results is indistinguishable from a good one. (c) The curator — the only component that removes/reorganizes skills — selects purely on RECENCY (unused > 30 days -> stale, > 90 -> archived) and on CONTENT overlap (opt-in consolidation, OFF by default), never on performance. The curator prompt even explicitly forbids reading value into usage counts: 'use=0 is not evidence a skill is valuable; it's absence of evidence either way.' So selection pressure exists (disuse and tidiness) but it is orthogonal to whether a change helped. The loop accumulates and occasionally garbage-collects by age; it never selects by fitness.
credit_assignment.status: not-implemented (searched skill_usage, curator, background_review, batch_runner; none found)

evidence.claim: 'Successful action' = the write tool-call returned OK, not measured impact
evidence.verbatim_artifact: Walks the review agent's session messages and collects successful memory and skill-management actions to surface to the user.

evidence.claim: Skill telemetry has only activity counters — no outcome/quality field
evidence.verbatim_artifact: def bump_view ... rec["view_count"] += 1 ; def bump_use ... rec["use_count"] += 1 ; def bump_patch ... rec["patch_count"] += 1

evidence.claim: Curator lifecycle = recency-based disuse, not performance
evidence.verbatim_artifact: active -> default; stale -> unused > stale_after_days; archived -> unused > archive_after_days; moved to .archive/

evidence.claim: Curator consolidation is content-overlap, OFF by default; usage counts explicitly NOT a value signal
evidence.verbatim_artifact: DEFAULT_CONSOLIDATE = False ... DO NOT use usage counters as a reason to skip consolidation ... 'use=0' is not evidence a skill is valuable; it's absence of evidence either way. Corollary: 'use=0' is ALSO not a reason to PRUNE a skill.

evidence.claim: Curator stale/archive thresholds are pure time-since-use
evidence.verbatim_artifact: DEFAULT_STALE_AFTER_DAYS = 30 ; DEFAULT_ARCHIVE_AFTER_DAYS = 90

persistence.finding: Persistence is strong and real — this is the one loop-stage that fully closes into the next session. Memory/skill writes go straight to disk stores (MEMORY.md/USER.md, ~/.hermes/skills/), injected into the next session's system prompt; skills are loadable by name. The curator never hard-deletes, only archives to a recoverable .archive/. So an improvement (or a bad write) persists and re-enters stage 1 as context. BUT because there's no fitness/credit stage, what persists is whatever was written, good or bad — the guardrails are all negative/incident-driven: a drift guard on edits (#26045), a loop guard so a failed memory write can't hang the turn (#42405), an opt-in human write-approval gate (default off) added because the background reviewer saved 'wrong assumptions', and a hardcoded DO-NOT-CAPTURE list in the review prompt (never save 'X tool is broken' style negative claims that 'harden into refusals'). These prevent known failure modes; none of them positively selects for improvements.
persistence.status: persistence default-on with memory/skills enabled; write-approval gate default-off (opt-in)

evidence.claim: Writes go straight to persistent stores and re-enter next session
evidence.verbatim_artifact: Writes go straight to the memory + skill stores. Main conversation and prompt cache are never touched.

evidence.claim: Curator never deletes, only archives (recoverable)
evidence.verbatim_artifact: Never auto-deletes — only archives. Archive is recoverable.

evidence.claim: Write-approval gate is human-in-loop and default-off; created because reviewer saved wrong assumptions
evidence.verbatim_artifact: background_review ... autonomously decides what to save (the source of the "wrong assumptions" users complained about) ... false (default) — write freely ... true — require approval

evidence.claim: Hardcoded negative-patch exclusion list to stop self-imposed refusals
evidence.verbatim_artifact: Do NOT capture ... Negative claims about tools or features ('browser tools do not work', 'X tool is broken') ... These harden into refusals the agent cites against itself for months after the actual problem was fixed.

hypothesis_verdict: confirmed. The hypothesis — 'Hermes only accumulates (memory/skills/trajectories pile up), it doesn't select; no fitness function, no credit-assignment gear turning accumulation into measured improvement' — holds against the code. Positive evidence for accumulation: (a) review prompt biases toward writing ('a no-op is a missed learning opportunity'); (b) memory add is append-only and skips the drift guard; (c) skill telemetry is counters-only with no outcome field; (d) the only selector (curator) prunes on age/disuse and reorganizes on content overlap (opt-in, off by default), explicitly refusing to read fitness into usage. Positive evidence for NO credit assignment: nowhere does the code measure whether a self-made change improved a later result. The nuance to state fairly (avoid over-claiming 'absolute'): Hermes DOES have real selection pressure — but it's recency/tidiness/incident-guard pressure, not fitness pressure; and it DOES have a within-session negative error signal (frustration/correction cues) — but not a cross-session outcome signal. So 'no selection at all' would overstate it; the precise claim is 'selection exists but is decoupled from whether the agent got better.'

weight_training_onramp.finding: The training pipeline is real but SEALED OFF from the live agent — it is a research tool fed by a curated dataset, not an auto-wire from real conversations. batch_runner requires --dataset_file, a researcher-curated JSONL where each entry has a 'prompt' field; it runs each prompt in a fresh agent with skip_memory=True (no persistent MEMORY/USER, and the memory/skill review triggers stay off) and save_trajectories=False (the batch harness writes its own trajectory outputs). trajectory_compressor is an offline post-processor: --input points at a JSONL file or a directory of them (the batch output or any curated set), compresses the middle of each trajectory to fit a token budget while 'preserving training signal', and writes new JSONL. It is not connected to any live session. save_trajectories (default False) only appends finished conversations to a local trajectory_samples.jsonl; nothing pipes that file into batch_runner. So the README's 'batch trajectory generation ... for training the next generation' describes a genuine curated-dataset generation+compression pipeline for training a future model — NOT an online loop where your real usage retrains the weights. skip_memory semantics in batch: don't load or write the persistent memory/skill stores, so batch runs are hermetic and reproducible (also used by the background review fork to avoid touching the parent cache).

evidence.claim: batch_runner consumes a curated dataset_file of prompts
evidence.verbatim_artifact: dataset_file (str): Path to the dataset JSONL file with 'prompt' field ... if not self.dataset_file.exists(): raise FileNotFoundError

evidence.claim: batch runs seal off memory and self-save trajectories
evidence.verbatim_artifact: save_trajectories=False,  # We handle saving ourselves ... skip_memory=True,  # Don't use persistent memory in batch runs

evidence.claim: trajectory_compressor is an offline file/dir post-processor preserving training signal
evidence.verbatim_artifact: Post-processes completed agent trajectories to compress them within a target token budget while preserving training signal quality. Usage: python trajectory_compressor.py --input=data/my_run

evidence.claim: save_trajectories default False; local file only
evidence.verbatim_artifact: save_trajectories: bool = False ... with open(filename, "a") ... f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")

evidence.claim: mini_swe_runner also outputs trajectories compatible with batch/compressor — a generator, no ground-truth scoring found
evidence.verbatim_artifact: Outputs trajectories in Hermes format ... compatible with the trajectory compression pipeline (searched for pass/resolved/score/reward; none found — it generates, does not grade).

completeness_note: Where I looked and what might still be missed: (1) I traced the self-improvement loop through agent/background_review.py, agent/curator.py, tools/skill_usage.py, tools/write_approval.py, tools/memory_tool.py, agent/turn_finalizer.py, agent/turn_context.py, agent/prompt_builder.py, agent/agent_init.py, batch_runner.py, trajectory_compressor.py, mini_swe_runner.py. (2) Honcho external dialectic user-modeling (README) is a memory PROVIDER plugin (plugins/memory, agent/memory_manager.py) — I confirmed it's wired as an external memory backend but did NOT audit whether Honcho itself computes any quality/dialectic score; if it does, that would be an external (not in-repo) fitness-ish signal worth a caveat. (3) I did not exhaustively read tools/skills_hub.py (4073 lines, hub install/sync) or tools/skills_guard.py (AST audit / trust levels) — these are safety/provenance guards, unlikely to contain fitness, but I only sampled them. (4) FTS5 session_search is a retrieval mechanism (recall), not evaluation — noted, not deep-dived. (5) I did NOT find any offline eval harness that gates runtime behavior; tests/ is unit tests (dev product). If a fitness signal exists, the most likely hiding places I did not fully rule out are: Honcho's internal scoring (external), and any reward/label field inside datagen-config-examples/ (I saw the dir but did not open the configs). Net: I'm confident no IN-REPO, RUNTIME credit-assignment gear exists; the residual uncertainty is external (Honcho) and in unopened datagen configs.
diagram_hints: Flywheel with 5 nodes. SOLID (code-provable) edges: [3 Actuators] -> [5 Persistence] (writes go straight to disk stores, background_review.py:5-8) -> re-injected into next session's context -> feeds [2 Error signal] (in-session frustration/correction cues, background_review.py:180-195). So the arc Actuator -> Persist -> Context -> in-session error-signal -> Actuator is a REAL closed sub-loop (accumulation loop). Also SOLID: a recency/tidiness pruning side-loop (curator: unused>30/90d -> archive; overlap -> consolidate) hanging off Persistence — draw it as a separate 'garbage-collect by age' arm, NOT as fitness. DASHED / MISSING (cannot be shown as implemented): [1 Fitness function] node — no target defined; and the [4 Credit-assignment] edge from Persistence/Actuator back to Fitness ('did this change help?') — this edge does not exist in code. Visual punchline: the ring is closed on the LEFT (write -> persist -> re-read -> write again) but broken on the RIGHT where a fitness/credit gear should sit — accumulation loop closes, improvement loop doesn't. Training onramp is a SEPARATE, DISCONNECTED box: curated dataset_file -> batch_runner (skip_memory, sealed) -> trajectory_compressor -> next-gen model weights; draw a dashed gap between the live agent's trajectory dump (local JSONL) and this box to show there is no auto-wire.
